preFix.title: Scandinavian rya rug: sampler on a frame loom
preFix.subtitle: Tying the ghiordes rya knot on a woven linen warp with plain-weave lock rows; sampler approximately 25 × 30 cm
preFix.excerpt: A rya-knot rug sampler showing the Scandinavian (ghiordes) knot structure on a backstrap or frame loom. Each row of rya knots is followed by 2 to 3 rows of plain-weave tabby to lock the knots in place. Pile length is 5 to 7 cm per knot bundle. The sampler is approximately 25 × 30 cm woven area and teaches the knot sequence, pile length control, and the tabby-locking method used in traditional Scandinavian rya rugs.
preFix.sourceNotes: Synthesised. The Scandinavian rya (ghiordes knot) weaving tradition is documented in the V&A textile collection and Nordic Folk Art Museum collections. No single public-domain text covers the specific sampler sequence described here; the knot structure itself (ghiordes) is a well-documented hand-knotting technique in the weaving literature.

content.text: The Scandinavian rya rug is built by alternating rows of
rya knots
with rows of plain-weave
tabby
. The knots provide the long pile; the tabby rows lock each row of knots into the warp structure. The characteristic shaggy surface comes from the pile length: traditional rya rugs have a
pile height
of 5 to 10 cm, much longer than most other knotted textiles. This sampler uses a pile height of 5 to 7 cm.

The sampler is worked on a frame loom (also called a rigid heddle loom or simple backstrap loom) with a linen warp. A frame loom suited to a 25 cm weaving width is enough for a sampler. The warp is set up as for plain weave: evenly spaced, one end per dent.
What you need

items.name: Frame loom, minimum 30 cm weaving width
Linen warp yarn, 4-ply or equivalent, approximately 8 ends per 5 cm
Pile yarn for rya knots: chunky wool yarn or rug wool in 2 to 3 colours
Weft yarn for tabby rows: linen or cotton, similar weight to warp
Gauge stick or ruler at pile height (6 cm), for consistent pile length
Sharp scissors for cutting pile bundles
Rug binding tape for hem finish (optional)

content.text: Method
Step 1: Warp the loom

Thread the linen warp onto the frame loom at 8 ends per 5 cm across a 25 cm width, giving 40 warp ends. Maintain even, moderate tension throughout warping: tight enough to allow the shed to open cleanly, not so tight that the frame bows. Tie off the warp ends securely.
Step 2: Hem header

Weave 4 to 6 rows of plain-weave tabby in the weft yarn before starting any rya knots. This builds the hem at the start of the sampler that will be folded under and stitched once the weaving is removed from the loom. Beat each row firmly with your fingers or a weaving comb.
Step 3: Tie the first row of rya knots

Cut the pile yarn into lengths of 12 to 14 cm (these will fold in half to give 5 to 7 cm of pile). Work the rya knot on each pair of adjacent warp ends across the full width. For each knot: fold a pile yarn length in half, place it behind two adjacent warps, bring the left end over the left warp and up between the two warps, and bring the right end over the right warp and up between the two warps. Pull both ends down to the planned pile length. Work from left to right across the full width, one knot per pair of warps.
Step 4: Weave tabby lock rows

After completing the rya knot row, weave 2 to 3 rows of plain-weave tabby using the weft yarn. Open the shed and pass the shuttle through; close the shed, beat the weft down so it sits directly on top of the knot bases. Open the alternate shed and return the shuttle. Repeat for 2 to 3 rows total. The tabby rows compress the knot bases and lock them firmly: without the tabby rows the knots will loosen and slide on the warp.
Step 5: Continue the sequence

Continue alternating rya knot rows and tabby lock rows for the full length of the sampler. Changing pile yarn colour at the start of any rya row is straightforward: simply switch to a new colour for the next knot across. The tabby rows between them allow colour bands and pattern variations. A sampler of 8 to 10 rya rows with tabby between will give a 25 to 30 cm woven length depending on how tightly the rows are beaten.
Step 6: Hem footer and remove from loom

Weave 4 to 6 rows of tabby at the end to match the starting hem header. Cut the warp ends from the loom, leaving at least 5 cm on each warp end. Tie the warp ends in pairs with overhand knots to secure the tabby header and footer. Fold the hem allowance to the back and stitch down, or apply rug binding tape over the hem on the back.
Pile variations

Pile height of 5 cm gives a flat, dense rya surface. Pile height of 7 to 10 cm gives the traditional Scandinavian shag look, which curves and shifts as the pile moves. Using multiple yarn colours within a single knot (threading two different colours together as one bundle) gives a blended tone in the pile. Cutting the pile to slightly different heights across a row creates a sculptural textured surface.
Common problems

items.fix: Always weave a minimum of 2 tabby rows between each knot row and beat them firmly. A third tabby row gives extra security on a loosely sett warp.
items.cause: Tabby lock rows were not woven tightly enough, or there were fewer than 2 tabby rows between knot rows
items.problem: Rya knots are loose and pulling out of the warp

items.fix: Cut all pile bundles to a consistent length before starting (12 to 14 cm for 6 cm pile). Use a gauge stick held against each finished knot to check pile height before moving to the next.
items.cause: Knot bundles were trimmed by eye rather than against a gauge
items.problem: Pile heights are inconsistent across the sampler

items.fix: Leave a slight arc or bow in the weft before beating it down: this allows the weft to curve slightly around each warp end without pulling the edge warps inward. Check the width after every 4 to 5 rows.
items.cause: The tabby weft is being pulled too tightly across the width on each pass
items.problem: Weaving is pulling in at the sides, narrowing as it progresses
